title: System Definition
content: A system is a set of elements interconnected in a way that produces its own pattern of behavior over time. Structure = Elements + Interconnections + Purpose. Elements are visible components; interconnections are relationships; purpose is the system's function.

title: Stocks and Flows
content: Stock is an accumulation (e.g., bugs in backlog). Inflow adds to stock; Outflow removes from stock. If Inflow > Outflow consistently, stock grows without bound. Key insight: stocks act as buffers and provide system memory.
tags: stocks flows inflow outflow accumulation buffer

title: Reinforcing Loop
content: Reinforcing (positive feedback) loop amplifies change in one direction — growth or collapse. Example: More sales → more revenue → more marketing budget → more sales. Can be virtuous (growth) or vicious (technical debt spiral).
tags: reinforcing loop positive feedback growth snowball amplification

title: Balancing Loop
content: Balancing (negative feedback) loop resists change and seeks a goal or equilibrium. Example: Room temperature deviates → thermostat activates → temperature returns to target. Drives stability and goal-seeking behavior.
tags: balancing loop negative feedback equilibrium stability thermostat

title: Leverage Points Level 1 — Parameters
content: Weakest leverage: Constants and parameters (numbers in the system). Example: changing a timeout from 30s to 60s. Easy to change but least impactful. Addresses symptoms not root cause.
tags: leverage points parameters constants weak intervention

title: Leverage Points Level 2 — Structure
content: Mid-strength leverage: Buffer sizes (stock capacity) and structure of stocks and flows. Changing system architecture falls here. Example: switching to async processing changes the flow structure fundamentally.
tags: leverage points structure stocks flows buffer capacity architecture

title: Leverage Points Level 3 — Feedback
content: Strong leverage: Strength of feedback loops; information flows; rules of the system (policies). Changing what information is available and when creates powerful behavioral shifts. Example: changing KPI from tickets closed to customer satisfaction.
tags: leverage points feedback loops information flows rules policies KPI

title: Leverage Points Level 4 — Paradigm
content: Strongest leverage: Goals of the system; mindset or paradigm; power to transcend paradigms. Changing the shared belief about why the system exists drives transformation. Example: shifting from output-focused to outcome-focused culture.
tags: leverage points paradigm mindset goals transformation highest impact

title: Archetype — Fixes that Fail
content: Short-term fix relieves symptom but creates unintended side effects that worsen the original problem over time. Solution: identify root cause; accept short-term pain; resist symptomatic fixes. Common in software: quick patches that accumulate technical debt.
tags: fixes that fail archetype workaround symptom root cause technical debt

title: Archetype — Shifting the Burden
content: Problem-solvers become dependent on symptomatic solution while fundamental solution atrophies. Example: adding more QA testers instead of fixing defect-prone development process. Solution: strengthen the fundamental solution path.
tags: shifting burden archetype dependency symptomatic fundamental solution

title: Archetype — Limits to Growth
content: Growth reinforcing loop encounters a balancing constraint that limits further growth. Example: user adoption grows then plateaus due to performance degradation. Solution: identify the limiting constraint and address it proactively.
tags: limits to growth archetype constraint ceiling growth plateau scalability

title: Archetype — Tragedy of the Commons
content: Multiple actors share a common resource; each acts in self-interest; resource is depleted for all. Example: shared database without query governance. Solution: mutual coercion through rules or quotas.
tags: tragedy commons archetype shared resource depletion governance quota

title: Archetype — Escalation
content: Two actors each respond to the other's actions by increasing their own. Creates runaway arms race. Example: competing teams building redundant features. Solution: negotiate de-escalation or unilateral restraint.
tags: escalation archetype arms race competition runaway feedback

title: Delay Patterns in Systems
content: Delays between action and response cause oscillation and policy resistance. Decision-makers over-correct because they don't see results immediately. Key principle: account for delays before adding more intervention. Delays are often invisible leverage points.
tags: delay oscillation response lag policy resistance overshoot intervention

title: Systems Thinking Diagnosis Checklist
content: Before solving: 1) Draw Stocks and Flows diagram; 2) Identify Reinforcing and Balancing loops; 3) Find Leverage Points (not just symptoms); 4) Check for unintended consequences; 5) Account for delays in the system.
tags: systems thinking checklist diagnosis stocks flows leverage unintended consequences
